citation: Lamplot JD, Petit C, Lee R, Mack CD, Herzog MM, Myer GD, Hammond K. Epidemiology of Stingers in the National Football League, 2015-2019. Sports Health. 2024. doi:10.1177/19417381231223413
abstract: This retrospective epidemiological study analyzed all in-game stingers (transient traumatic neuropraxia of the brachial plexus or cervical nerve root) recorded in the NFL electronic health record system from 2015 to 2019. A total of 691 in-game stingers occurred over 5 seasons (mean 138.2/year). Incidence rate was 11.48 per 100,000 player-plays overall, higher during the regular season (12.26) than preseason (8.87). Running backs (18.00) and linebackers (15.87) had the highest incidence by position. The average single-season injury risk was 3.74%. Overall, 76.41% of athletes missed no additional time; mean time missed was 4.79 days. Concomitant injuries were uncommon (7.09%), most frequently shoulder injuries, cervical spine injuries, and concussions.
tldr: Over five NFL seasons (2015-2019), stingers occurred at a stable rate of ~138 per year and were most common in running backs and linebackers during regular-season games. The vast majority of affected players missed no additional time beyond the game of injury. While concomitant injuries were rare, concussion and cervical spine injuries did occur and must be ruled out before return to play — a critical reminder for sideline medical staff.
methods: Retrospective epidemiology study. All in-game stinger injuries (coded as 'neck brachial plexus stretch' or 'neck brachial plexus compression') from the NFL centralized electronic health record system were aggregated for the 2015-2019 seasons across all 32 clubs. Injury data were integrated with NFL Game Statistical and Information System (GSIS) play-participation data. Incidence rates per 100,000 player-plays were calculated using the Poisson distribution. Descriptive statistics and Poisson regression were used; significance accepted at p < 0.05.
findings: 691 total in-game stingers over 5 seasons; average single-season risk 3.74%. Regular-season incidence (12.26/100,000 player-plays) significantly exceeded preseason (8.87, p < 0.01). Highest incidence among running backs (18.00), linebackers (15.87), and defensive linemen (14.78). 76.41% of athletes missed no additional time; mean time missed was 4.79 days. Brachial plexus compression injuries resulted in longer mean time loss (5.98 days) than stretch injuries (4.20 days). Recurrence history had no effect on time missed. Concomitant injuries occurred in 7.09%: shoulder (40%), cervical spine (31%), concussion (22%).
limitations: Findings may not generalize to collegiate, high school, or youth football athletes. Underreporting is likely given the injury's transient nature and players' career concerns. The number of in-game plays missed during the injury game was not available. Player age was not available, precluding age-mechanism analysis. Cervical nerve root compression as a distinct mechanism could not be isolated due to EHR coding structure. MRI and imaging data were not available. Permanent neurologic deficits could not be assessed; only time-missed data were available. Injury recurrence before NFL participation was not captured.
practicalImplications: Medical staff should be prepared to efficiently perform concurrent concussion and cervical spine injury evaluations whenever a stinger is diagnosed, as co-occurring injuries — though uncommon — do occur. Understanding that most NFL stingers resolve without time loss can assist clinicians in managing player and coaching staff expectations. Running backs and linebackers represent the highest-risk positions and may benefit from targeted neck-strengthening and contact-technique education.
athleteDev: Running backs and linebackers sustain stingers at more than double the rate of quarterbacks and wide receivers, attributable to high-velocity collision patterns. Position-specific training that strengthens the cervical musculature and refines contact mechanics — particularly for players who initiate or absorb high-velocity collisions — may reduce stinger incidence and recurrence over a career.
rtp: Return to play after a stinger requires complete resolution of symptoms including full painless cervical range of motion and full extremity strength. Concurrent concussion evaluation is mandated by NFL protocol when a stinger is diagnosed. Cervical spine injury must also be cleared before clearance. The 2018 NFL helmet-lowering rule did not appear to reduce stinger incidence, highlighting the continued need for RTP vigilance in high-contact positions.

citation: Knapik DM, Mack CD, Lee R, Heersink W, Tanenbaum JE, Wetzel RJ, Voos JE. Epidemiology of Tibial Fractures in Professional American Football Athletes From 2013 to 2019. Orthop J Sports Med. 2021;9(11):23259671211040878. doi:10.1177/23259671211040878
abstract: This retrospective case series reviewed the NFL injury database (ISS 2013-2014; EHR 2015-2019) to identify all tibial fractures over seven seasons. A total of 64 fractures in 60 athletes were identified. Median time lost was 74 days. Defensive secondary athletes had the highest proportion (16%), followed by running backs (14%). The majority (61%) occurred during regular-season games, predominantly in the final 4 weeks (weeks 13-17). Being tackled with a direct impact to the leg was the most common mechanism. Lateral tibial plateau fractures were most frequent; distal tibial fractures caused the greatest time loss (mean 175 days). Surgery was required in 48% of EHR-era injuries; surgical cases missed a median of 232 days versus 56 days for conservatively managed cases.
tldr: Over seven NFL seasons, tibial fractures were uncommon but highly consequential, causing a median of 74 days lost and up to nearly a full year for surgically managed cases. Defensive backs and running backs were most affected, with most fractures occurring during the final four weeks of the regular season via direct contact during tackling. This late-season clustering points to fatigue-related neuromuscular breakdown as a likely contributor, underscoring the value of load management and neuromuscular maintenance late in the season.
methods: Retrospective case series using the NFL Injury Surveillance System (ISS) for 2013-2014 and the centralized electronic health record (EHR) system for 2015-2019. All tibial fractures were identified; purely ligamentous injuries and bony contusions without definite fracture were excluded. Variables collected included athlete characteristics, injury timing, fracture type and characteristics, associated injuries, management (operative vs conservative), and days missed. Surgical procedures were identified via CPT codes reviewed by an IQVIA analytics team. Descriptive statistics were calculated with SPSS v23.
findings: 64 tibial fractures in 60 athletes; median days lost 74 (IQR 34-153). Defensive secondary (16%) and running backs (14%) had highest injury counts. 61% occurred during regular-season games; 51% of those in weeks 13-17. Most common activity: being tackled (27%); most common mechanism: direct impact (58%). Lateral tibial plateau fractures were most common (46%); distal tibial fractures caused greatest time loss (mean 175 days). 48% of EHR-era injuries required surgery; surgical cases lost median 232 days vs 56 days for conservative management. Open fractures occurred in 4%; intra-articular involvement in 43% of distal tibial fractures.
limitations: Retrospective design depends on completeness of NFL ISS and EHR documentation. Data quality improved after EHR launch, so 2013-2014 season data (ISS) were excluded from surgical and RTP analyses. Days missed was unavailable in 36% of injuries. Data on athletes who left the NFL after fracture were not collected. Overall incidence rates by position exposure were not calculated. Mechanism and activity data were entered by athletic trainers without systematic video validation. Imaging and operative reports were not available for review. Results are specific to professional football and may not generalize to other sports or competition levels.
practicalImplications: Medical staff should have high suspicion for concomitant ligamentous and meniscal injuries in athletes with lateral tibial plateau fractures given the 29% co-injury rate. For fractures amenable to intramedullary nailing, earlier return-to-sport can be anticipated versus those requiring ORIF. Late-season fatigue-related injury clustering suggests value in monitoring neuromuscular readiness in the final weeks of the season to reduce severe lower extremity injury risk.
athleteDev: Defensive secondary and running back athletes face the highest tibial fracture risk, reflecting the open-field tackling demands of these positions. Position-specific conditioning that emphasizes neuromuscular control, dynamic balance, and proper tackling mechanics — especially during the high-fatigue late-season period — may reduce fracture risk. Monitoring cumulative load and incorporating deload strategies in weeks 12-17 may be warranted.
rtp: Tibial fractures carry significant RTP timelines: median 74 days overall, 232 days for surgical cases. Intramedullary nailing has demonstrated 90% RTP rates with longer career longevity than ORIF. Distal tibial fractures with intra-articular extension and concomitant fibular fracture carry the longest RTP timeline. Staged functional rehabilitation with clearance criteria for full sport participation is critical given the high rate of surgical intervention in this population.

citation: Hoge C, Sabbagh R, Morgan M, Grawe BM. Epidemiology of Youth and High School American Football-Related Injuries Presenting to United States Emergency Departments: 2010-2019. Physician Sportsmed. 2021. doi:10.1080/00913847.2021.1931980
abstract: This cross-sectional retrospective study queried the NEISS database (2010-2019) to compare football-related ED injury characteristics and rates in youth (ages 6-12) and high school (ages 14-18) athletes. A total of 2,827,751 weighted national estimates were identified from 89,236 unique cases. High school athletes accounted for 59.87% of injuries. The overall ED injury risk was 16.17 per 1,000 participants/year for high school athletes versus 11.97 for youth. Both groups showed significant decreases in injury risk over the decade (p < 0.0001). Head/neck and hand/wrist injuries were most common in both groups. High school athletes were almost twice as likely to present with knee injuries and 2.37 times more likely with shoulder injuries. Youth athletes were 2.5 times more likely to sustain shoulder fractures while high school athletes had over 5 times the risk of dislocations.
tldr: Across a decade of national ED surveillance data, football injury risk for both youth and high school athletes significantly declined — suggesting that rule changes, equipment improvements, and coaching technique initiatives since 2010 are showing measurable impact. However, high school athletes carry a substantially higher overall injury burden, with nearly double the concussion risk and over 5 times the dislocation risk compared to youth players. Injury prevention efforts must be age-group-specific, targeting knee and shoulder injuries in high school athletes and fractures in youth players.
methods: Cross-sectional retrospective study querying the Consumer Product Safety Commission's NEISS database from 2010-2019 for football-related injuries (Product Code 1211) in ages 6-12 (youth) and 14-18 (high school). Age 13 was excluded to minimize overlap. National weighted estimates and 95% CIs were calculated using IBM SPSS v27. Chi-square with odds ratios assessed bivariate associations. Poisson regression evaluated trends in injury risk, participation counts, and weighted estimates over time. Injury risk was calculated as injuries per 1,000 participants/year using SFIA (youth) and NFHS (high school) participation data.
findings: 2,827,751 weighted national estimates of football ED injuries (2010-2019); high school athletes comprised 59.87%. Overall injury risk: 16.17/1,000/year (high school) vs 11.97/1,000/year (youth). Both groups showed significant annual decreases (5.27%/year youth; 4.92%/year high school; p < 0.0001). Head/neck and hand/wrist were the most common injury sites in both groups. High school athletes were ~2x more likely to present with knee injuries and 2.37x more likely with shoulder injuries per 1,000/year. Youth athletes were 2.5x more likely to sustain shoulder fractures; high school athletes were ~10x more likely to sustain shoulder dislocations. High school athletes had almost double concussion risk and over 5x the dislocation risk compared to youth per year.
limitations: NEISS only captures injuries presenting to EDs, underrepresenting total injury burden and emphasizing more severe injuries. The database codes only the most severe injury per encounter; multiple injuries in one visit are not captured. Participation data (SFIA/NFHS) account only for core or team participants, not recreational players, potentially exaggerating injury rates. NFHS participation data were unavailable for 2019-2020 due to COVID-19. The study is limited to ages 6-12 and 14-18, excluding age 13.
practicalImplications: The declining injury risk trend since 2010 provides support for continued investment in rule changes, improved equipment, and coaching education as effective public health interventions. However, the persistent age-related differences in injury patterns — particularly the high school athletes' elevated concussion and dislocation risk — indicate that prevention programming must be age-differentiated. High school programs should emphasize shoulder and knee stability, while youth programs should focus on bone health and fracture-risk awareness.
athleteDev: Youth football athletes are at higher relative risk for skeletal fractures (including shoulder fractures) due to open physes and immature neuromuscular control, while high school athletes have greater risk for ligamentous and soft tissue injuries such as dislocations and sprains consistent with skeletally maturing bodies. Developmental programming should account for these physiological differences, incorporating age-appropriate contact progressions, movement skill development, and load management.
rtp: This study does not directly address RTP timelines, but the high rates of concussion, knee, and shoulder injuries in high school football players — conditions associated with significant recovery periods — underscore the importance of structured RTP protocols at the youth and scholastic levels. Concussion rates were nearly double in high school compared to youth athletes, making standardized concussion assessment and graduated RTP protocols especially critical at the high school level.

citation: Binney ZO, Bachynski KE. Estimating the prevalence at death of CTE neuropathology among professional football players. Neurology. 2019;92:43-45. doi:10.1212/WNL.0000000000006699
abstract: This brief methodological analysis used bias analysis to estimate the plausible range of CTE prevalence at death among former NFL players, contextualizing the widely-reported 99% finding from a 2017 JAMA brain bank study (Mez et al.). The authors obtained a fully enumerated cohort of 1,142 NFL players who died between February 2008 and May 2016 from Pro-Football-Reference.com. By modeling the selection bias inherent in voluntary brain donation — where symptomatic players are more likely to donate — the estimated true CTE prevalence ranges from 9.6% to near 100% depending on the assumed donation probability among those with CTE. At a 50% donation probability, prevalence is estimated at 19.3%; at 90%, it is 10.7%. CTE prevalence remains high under most plausible scenarios.
tldr: The frequently cited '99% of NFL players have CTE' statistic from the largest brain bank study is heavily influenced by selection bias, as symptomatic players are far more likely to donate their brains. After correcting for this bias across a fully enumerated cohort of 1,142 deceased NFL players, estimated CTE prevalence ranges from approximately 10% to near 100% depending on the strength of the selection effect. Even at conservative assumptions, CTE prevalence in deceased professional football players remains meaningfully elevated — a fact that carries important implications for long-term athlete health monitoring and policy.
methods: Bias analysis applied to the Mez et al. (2017) JAMA brain bank study. A complete list of 1,142 former NFL players who died during the study timeframe (February 2008 to May 2016) was obtained from Pro-Football-Reference.com as the fully enumerated eligible cohort. The selection effect was quantified using two of three possible bias parameters: (1) the probability that a player with CTE donated his brain, and (3) the ratio of donation probability among CTE cases versus non-cases. These were used to calculate corrected CTE prevalence estimates across a range of plausible selection scenarios and graphed as a sensitivity curve.
findings: Estimated CTE prevalence ranges from 9.6% to near 100% depending on the proportion of CTE cases captured by the brain bank. Assuming 50% of CTE cases donated their brains yields an estimated prevalence of 19.3%, with those having CTE being 461 times more likely to donate than those without. Assuming 90% of CTE cases donated yields a prevalence of 10.7%. Under most plausible scenarios, CTE prevalence in deceased NFL players remains high. The uncorrected (unadjusted) prevalence from the Mez et al. study was 99% (110/111 NFL players).
limitations: This analysis does not estimate CTE prevalence in college or semi-professional players, where rates may be lower. The proportion of CTE neuropathology cases who exhibit neurologic symptoms while alive remains unclear, complicating interpretation. If CTE causes earlier death on average, CTE cases may still be overrepresented even after this correction. The analysis is based on only 111 retired NFL players with donated brains. The most probable strength of the selection effect is not estimated; only a range is provided.
practicalImplications: The true prevalence of CTE in professional football players is substantially uncertain due to the voluntary, symptomatic nature of brain donation. While the 99% headline figure is likely an overestimate due to selection bias, corrected estimates still suggest meaningfully elevated CTE risk in this population. Organizations responsible for athlete long-term health should not dismiss CTE risk based on the over-inflation argument, and continued investment in in-vivo CTE biomarker development and longitudinal cognitive monitoring of former players is warranted.
athleteDev: This study does not directly address athlete development during playing careers. However, its finding that CTE risk — even after selection-bias correction — may affect a meaningful proportion of professional football players supports efforts to reduce cumulative head impact exposure during player development, including limiting full-contact practice, minimizing helmet-to-helmet contact in technique training, and implementing progressive contact exposure models across developmental levels.
rtp: This study does not address return to play from acute injury. However, the elevated CTE prevalence estimates — even after conservative bias correction — reinforce the importance of stringent concussion RTP protocols and cumulative head impact monitoring across a professional career. The findings support the use of longitudinal cognitive baseline testing and post-career neurological monitoring programs as components of comprehensive athlete health management.

citation: Guneşliol BE, Baş M. Evaluation of Dietary Intake and Body Composition of Collegiate American Football Players. Progress in Nutrition. 2021;23(4):e2021198. doi:10.23751/pn.v23i4.11548
abstract: This observational study evaluated the dietary intake and body composition of 185 collegiate American football players in Ankara, Turkey by position group (defensive linemen [DL, n=22], offensive linemen [OL, n=23], defensive backs [DB, n=71], offensive backs [OB, n=34], and receivers [R, n=35]) during the 2017-2018 mid-season. Body composition was assessed by anthropometric measurements and bioelectrical impedance analysis (BIA). Dietary intake was assessed by 3-day diet records. Energy, carbohydrate, and protein intakes of DL and OL were significantly lower than receivers. Players consumed high fat, dietary cholesterol, and sodium but were low in carbohydrates and potassium. Average BMI and body fat percentage of DL (31.38 kg/m² and 26.13%) and OL (32.95 kg/m² and 30.06%) were significantly higher than other position groups. Most DL and OL were classified as obese.
tldr: Collegiate American football players — particularly linemen — follow nutritionally unbalanced diets marked by insufficient carbohydrate and protein intake relative to recommendations, alongside excessive fat, saturated fat, and sodium. Most DL and OL are obese by BMI and body fat percentage standards, elevating cardiovascular and metabolic disease risk. These findings call for individualized nutritional education and position-specific dietary strategies, especially for linemen who face the greatest long-term cardiometabolic risk.
methods: Observational study of 185 collegiate American football players from five university-level teams in Ankara, Turkey (2016-2017 season, measured mid-season 2017-2018). Players were categorized into five position groups: DL, OL, DB, OB, and R. Body composition was assessed via anthropometric measurements (InBody 570, Seca 213 stadiometer) including BMI, waist circumference, hip circumference, WHR, and WHtR, plus BIA measurements (BFP, BFM, LBM, TBW, BMR) using InBody 570. Dietary intake was determined via 3-day diet records analyzed using the computer-aided Turkish Nutrition Information System (NIS). Statistical analysis used IBM SPSS v24, with ANOVA and Tukey HSD (or Tamhane's T2) for group comparisons; significance at p < 0.05.
findings: Energy and carbohydrate intakes of DL and OL were significantly lower than R (p < 0.001). Protein intakes of DL and OL were also significantly lower than R (p < 0.001). Most players (88.1%) consumed carbohydrates below sports nutrition recommendations (5-7 g/kg/day); 95.1% consumed fiber below AI. All players consumed SFAs above recommended levels; omega-6/omega-3 ratios were above recommended in 97.3% of players. Most players consumed sodium above RDA and potassium below RDA. BMI and BFP of DL and OL were significantly higher than other groups; most DL and OL were obese. Average BFPs for DL: 26.13%; OL: 30.06%; DB: 16.81%; OB: 15.69%; R: 12.79%. Average LBMs of DL and OL were significantly higher than other groups.
limitations: Daily physical activity records were not collected, precluding calculation of individual energy requirements. Dietary data were self-reported by participants via diet records, which may include inaccuracies in portion size estimation. Data collection occurred during the season when tight schedules limited more objective dietary assessment methods. The study population was Turkish collegiate players, limiting direct generalizability to American collegiate or professional players. This is reportedly the first study evaluating dietary intake of collegiate American football players by position group in Turkey.
practicalImplications: Position-specific nutritional strategies are needed for collegiate football players, with particular urgency for linemen (DL and OL) who show the highest obesity rates and lowest relative energy and carbohydrate intakes. Nutrition education should emphasize adequate carbohydrate intake (5-7 g/kg/day) for performance and recovery, increased whole-food protein sources, reduced saturated fat and processed sodium intake, and improved omega-6/omega-3 balance through food-first strategies (fish, walnuts, green leafy vegetables). Monitoring cardiometabolic health markers in linemen throughout their collegiate careers is advisable.
athleteDev: Body composition profiles differ substantially by position in collegiate football. Linemen carry significantly higher body fat percentages and BMI — elevating long-term cardiometabolic risk — while skill-position players (DB, OB, R) maintain body compositions closer to athletic norms. Strength and conditioning staff should integrate body composition monitoring and individualized nutrition programming into athlete development plans, particularly for linemen who may need to prioritize body fat reduction alongside lean mass maintenance to protect long-term health.
rtp: This study does not directly address return to play from injury. However, inadequate carbohydrate and protein intake in linemen — the two most critical macronutrients for tissue repair and recovery — may impair recovery from injury or overuse. Nutritional optimization, particularly adequate carbohydrate availability and protein intake above the 1.6-1.7 g/kg/day recommendation for American football players, should be a component of any comprehensive injury recovery program.
